title: Water-leaf and sized paper
subtitle: Why handmade paper is sized, what sizing does, and how to choose an adhesive
excerpt: A freshly-pulled handmade sheet is 'water-leaf': fully absorbent, with no barrier between the fibres and liquids. Water-leaf paper takes watercolour immediately but feathers ink badly. Sizing fills the fibre gaps with a thin film that controls absorbency. This article covers what sizing does, how to choose between gelatin, methyl cellulose, and starch, and when to leave paper unsized.
sourceNotes: Winifred Lutz and Timothy Barrett, 'Surface Sizing of Handmade Papers', Paper Conservation Catalog (1994, AIC Book and Paper Group). Also draws on Dard Hunter, Papermaking: The History and Technique of an Ancient Craft (1943, Knopf).

glossaryTerms.definition: An unsized handmade sheet: fully absorbent, with no sizing applied to the fibres. Water-leaf paper takes up liquids immediately but doesn't hold pen or brush lines without feathering.

Applying a size solution to the surface of a finished sheet, as opposed to 'beater sizing' where size is added to the pulp before sheet formation. Surface sizing gives more even coverage and is more reversible.
Dipping a sheet in a warm dilute gelatin solution to deposit a thin film between surface fibres. The traditional sizing method for writing paper in Europe from the 14th century onward.

content.text: Fresh from the mould, every handmade sheet is

: the fibres sit loosely together with nothing between them except dried pulp. A drop of ink on water-leaf spreads along the fibre channels in all directions. Sizing fills those channels. How much sizing changes the paper's character, and each size has a different feel.

Unsized water-leaf paper is correct for: watercolour and wash (where you want fast absorption into the sheet rather than pooling on the surface), Suminagashi marbling (unsized paper absorbs the floating ink pattern immediately), and packaging sheets. Do not size paper that will be used as an absorbent art material. Sizing is for writing paper, printmaking paper that needs ink to sit cleanly on the surface, and paper that will be printed on.
The traditional European size is animal gelatin dissolved in hot water.

gives a slightly stiff, smooth sheet that holds a pen line well. The bath is made at about 2% gelatin by weight (2 g per 100 ml water) and used warm (50°C). Papers are dipped, one at a time, allowed to drip, then dried flat. Gelatin sizing is reversed by soaking in warm water, making it archivally acceptable. The main drawback is that the bath needs to stay warm for the whole session.

Methyl cellulose (MC) powder mixed to a 2-3% solution with cold water gives a thin, brushable size. It is acid-free, reversible, and easier to use at room temperature than gelatin. Apply with a wide soft brush over a dry sheet; two thin coats give better coverage than one thick coat. MC gives a slightly silkier surface feel than gelatin, and is the first choice for conservation-grade sizing of old or fragile sheets where immersion is not practical.

Cooked starch paste diluted heavily with water (1 part paste to 8-10 parts water) gives a traditional East-Asian-style size. The finished sheet is slightly warmer in tone and more flexible than gelatin-sized paper. Starch sizing is used in Japanese papermaking where the sheet is brush-dried on a heated board; the starch locks the fibres in position as it dries. For home use, dilute wheat-paste can be applied by dipping or brushing onto dry sheets and dried flat under light weight.

To test whether a sheet has enough size, write a test word with an iron-gall or dip-pen ink and let it dry. If the letters feather at the edges, the sheet needs more size or a second dip. If the ink beads and does not absorb at all, the size is too heavy, dilute the bath and re-size or dip only briefly. A correctly-sized sheet absorbs ink within a second or two and holds a clean edge.
